text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.
I’m locating the catalog page and the current data-fetching path first so I can wire the sync form into the right cache behavior without regressing page responsiveness.
I found a very small Next app, so I’m reading the page and data layer now to adapt the caching pattern directly where products are loaded.

The app is minimal. I’m checking the current Next cache API for this canary version so the implementation matches the intended stale-while-revalidate behavior instead of forcing blocking invalidation.

I have the cache model pinned down now. I’m editing three places: enable Cache Components, tag the product query with `products`, and add an inline Server Action that starts a non-blocking sync job and invalidates that tag with stale-while-revalidate semantics once the sync finishes.
The core change is in place. I’m running a build now to catch any Next-specific issues around inline Server Actions, cache tags, and the page’s async server component.

text: The build failure is straightforward: the page needs to be an async server component. I’m fixing that and re-running the build.

text: The build passes now. I’m checking the final diff so I can call out the actual behavior change precisely, including the small TypeScript config update Next applied during build.

Implemented the admin catalog page with cached product reads and a non-blocking sync flow.

`getAllProducts()` in [lib/db.js](/home/user/workspace/lib/db.js:1) now uses `"use cache"`, `cacheTag("products")`, and `cacheLife("minutes")` so catalog browsing stays fast and shared across views. The sync path updates the underlying product data in the background, then calls `revalidateTag("products", "max")`, which keeps requests responsive and lets dependent views converge shortly after without forcing immediate blocking recomputation.

The page in [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx:1) is now an async server component with a regular HTML `<form>` whose `action` is an inline Server Action. Submitting `Sync latest catalog` starts the background sync and returns immediately. I also enabled Cache Components in [next.config.ts](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts:1), which this cache API requires.

Verified with `npm run build`. Next also updated [tsconfig.json](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json:1) to include `.next/dev/types/**/*.ts`.
